---
title: "Client Report - Missing values"
subtitle: "Unit 2 Task 3"
author: "Vincent Gentry"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import requests #new for reading in JSON
import io       #new for reading in JSON
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))
```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Convert values to missing__ Start with a fresh copy of the flights dataset by reading it in from the link provided above (as opposed to starting with the dataset you ended with in the last assignment). Identify data values that are problematic, non-sense or impossible and change them to be missing. In your report include one record example (one row) from your clean data, in the raw JSON format. Your example should display at least one missing value so that we can verify it was done correctly. (Note: JSON will convert NaN's to null). Describe your process for finding values that needed to be changed, and how you changed them.

I read the JSON file again from the link and checked each column for bad values.

I changed these values to missing (`null`):

1. Bad month names in `month` (like `n/a` and `Febuary`).
2. Text values in `num_of_delays_carrier` that are not true numbers (like `1500+`).
3. Negative delay values, because delay counts and delay minutes cannot be negative.

I then printed one cleaned row in raw JSON format that includes at least one `null` value.

```{python}
import json

# Start from a fresh copy of the source data
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))

# Rule-based cleaning: convert problematic/impossible values to null
valid_months = [
    "January", "February", "March", "April", "May", "June",
    "July", "August", "September", "October", "November", "December"
]

df_clean = df.with_columns([
    # Invalid month labels become null
    pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(valid_months))
      .then(pl.col("month"))
      .otherwise(None)
      .alias("month"),

    # Coerce numeric-like strings; invalid strings (for example "1500+") become null
    pl.col("num_of_delays_carrier")
      .cast(pl.Float64, strict=False)
      .cast(pl.Int64, strict=False)
      .alias("num_of_delays_carrier"),

    # Impossible negative values become null
    pl.when(pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") < 0)
      .then(None)
      .otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft"))
      .alias("num_of_delays_late_aircraft"),

    pl.when(pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas") < 0)
      .then(None)
      .otherwise(pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas"))
      .alias("minutes_delayed_nas"),
])

# Quick audit: missing values after cleaning
df_clean.null_count()

# One example cleaned record in raw JSON format with at least one null
row_with_null = df_clean.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal(*[pl.col(c).is_null() for c in df_clean.columns])
).head(1)

example_json = json.dumps(row_with_null.to_dicts()[0], indent=2, ensure_ascii=False)
print(example_json)


```

## QUESTION 2

2. __Filling in missing data__ For all the "minutes_delayed" columns, fill in any missing values with the median of that column. For the "num_of" columns, fill in missing values with that column's mean.


```{python}
# Fill missing values according to instructions:
# - minutes_delayed* columns -> median
# - num_of* columns -> mean

minutes_cols = [c for c in df_clean.columns if c.startswith("minutes_delayed")]
num_of_cols = [c for c in df_clean.columns if c.startswith("num_of")]

df_filled = df_clean.with_columns(
  [pl.col(c).fill_null(pl.col(c).median()).alias(c) for c in minutes_cols] +
  [pl.col(c).fill_null(pl.col(c).mean()).alias(c) for c in num_of_cols]
)

df_filled.null_count()


```

## QUESTION 3
 
3. __Display a histogram for num_of_delays_carrier and for minutes_delayed_nas (2 separate histograms in total). Comment on the shape of each one. Also report the mean of each of these columns now that missing values have been filled in.__

Both histograms are right-skewed. Most rows are in lower values, with fewer rows in very high values.

- `num_of_delays_carrier` is right-skewed.
- `minutes_delayed_nas` is also right-skewed and has a longer tail.

Means after filling missing values:

- Mean of `num_of_delays_carrier` = **691.29**
- Mean of `minutes_delayed_nas` = **69,682.66**

```{python}
# Compute required means after filling
means = df_filled.select([
  pl.col("num_of_delays_carrier").mean().alias("mean_num_of_delays_carrier"),
  pl.col("minutes_delayed_nas").mean().alias("mean_minutes_delayed_nas")
])
means

# Build a plain Python dict for plotting (avoids pandas/pyarrow dependency)
plot_df = {
  "num_of_delays_carrier": df_filled["num_of_delays_carrier"].to_list(),
  "minutes_delayed_nas": df_filled["minutes_delayed_nas"].to_list(),
}

# Histogram 1: num_of_delays_carrier
p1 = (
  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x="num_of_delays_carrier"))
  + geom_histogram(bins=30, fill="#2A9D8F", color="white")
  + labs(
    title="Histogram of num_of_delays_carrier",
    x="Number of Carrier Delays",
    y="Count"
  )
)

# Histogram 2: minutes_delayed_nas
p2 = (
  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x="minutes_delayed_nas"))
  + geom_histogram(bins=30, fill="#E76F51", color="white")
  + labs(
    title="Histogram of minutes_delayed_nas",
    x="Minutes Delayed (NAS)",
    y="Count"
  )
)

from IPython.display import display
display(p1)
display(p2)


```
